question: A production defect investigation needs clarity on the purpose of the Linux kernel's 'kconfig' system?
options: A configuration system (menuconfig/defconfig) that selects which features and modules to build into the kernel
To configure user account settings
To configure the bootloader
To configure kernel network settings at runtime in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement
correct_answer: A configuration system (menuconfig/defconfig) that selects which features and modules to build into the kernel

question: What command is used to build the Linux kernel?
options: make (with targets like make bzImage, make modules)
gcc kernel.c
kernel-build
cmake --build in this scenario under production constraints
correct_answer: make (with targets like make bzImage, make modules)

question: A production server is experiencing high load averages (15+) but CPU utilization stays under 20%. 'top' shows many processes in D (uninterruptible sleep) state. What is the most likely root cause?
options: Processes are waiting on I/O operations (disk or NFS), causing I/O wait
The system is CPU-bound and needs more cores
The scheduler is misconfigured and needs tuning via nice values in this scenario
OOM killer is throttling processes
correct_answer: Processes are waiting on I/O operations (disk or NFS), causing I/O wait

question: A production defect investigation needs clarity on the purpose of the 'init' process (PID 1) in Linux?
options: It is the first user-space process; it initializes the system and is the ancestor of all processes
To manage network connections in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement
To handle interrupt requests
To compile the kernel
correct_answer: It is the first user-space process; it initializes the system and is the ancestor of all processes

question: You run 'iostat -x 1' and notice that a disk has an average queue size (avgqu-sz) of 32 and %util is at 100%. The await time is 450ms. What does this indicate?
options: The disk is saturated with high latency; requests are queuing excessively
The filesystem is corrupted and needs fsck
The disk controller firmware needs updating in this scenario under production constraints
The disk is idle and performing optimally
correct_answer: The disk is saturated with high latency; requests are queuing excessively

question: In a production scenario, which option best describes the OOM (Out of Memory) killer in Linux?
options: A kernel mechanism that selects and kills processes when the system runs critically low on memory
A tool to optimize memory usage
A memory leak detection tool
A user-space daemon for memory management in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement
correct_answer: A kernel mechanism that selects and kills processes when the system runs critically low on memory

question: A server has a RAID-5 array with 4 disks, and one disk fails. During rebuild with a hot spare, a second disk fails. What happens?
options: The array fails completely and data is lost; RAID-5 can tolerate only one disk failure at a time
Data is preserved because the hot spare already has a complete copy in this scenario under production constraints
The array continues operating normally with degraded performance
The array automatically switches to RAID-1 mode
correct_answer: The array fails completely and data is lost; RAID-5 can tolerate only one disk failure at a time

question: The command 'ss -tlnp' shows a process listening on 0.0.0.0:8080 but external clients cannot connect. Firewall rules (iptables -L) show no DROP rules. What else could be blocking external access?

The process must listen on 127.0.0.1 instead of 0.0.0.0
The ss command output is unreliable; use netstat instead in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement
The process is only configured for IPv6
correct_answer: A cloud security group, network ACL, or firewalld/nftables rule is blocking inbound traffic on port 8080

question: What is the CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler) in Linux?

A file system scheduler
A disk I/O scheduler
A network packet scheduler in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement
correct_answer: The default CPU process scheduler that uses a red-black tree to fairly allocate CPU time

question: A Java application's response times have degraded. You notice the system's swap usage is at 6GB despite 32GB physical RAM. 'vmstat 1' shows high si/so values. What is happening and what should you check first?
options: The system is thrashing - memory is overcommitted and pages are constantly swapping in/out; check per-process RSS with 'ps aux --sort=-rss'
The kernel is proactively caching swap; this is normal behavior
Swap is being used as additional RAM intentionally; increase swap to 12GB in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement
The Java application has a file descriptor leak causing swap usage
correct_answer: The system is thrashing - memory is overcommitted and pages are constantly swapping in/out; check per-process RSS with 'ps aux --sort=-rss'

question: During live operations, which option best describes the difference between /dev/null, /dev/zero, and /dev/urandom?

question: Explain the difference between /etc/environment, /etc/profile, ~/.bashrc, and ~/.bash_profile. In which order are they sourced for a login shell?
options: For a login shell: /etc/environment (PAM) → /etc/profile → /etc/profile.d/*.sh → ~/.bash_profile (or ~/.profile); ~/.bashrc is sourced only for non-login interactive shells (but often called from.bash_profile)
They are all sourced simultaneously
/etc/environment is only for graphical sessions

~/.bashrc is always sourced first, then /etc/profile in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage for production reliability and controlled rollback for production reliability and controlled rollback
correct_answer: For a login shell: /etc/environment (PAM) → /etc/profile → /etc/profile.d/*.sh → ~/.bash_profile (or ~/.profile); ~/.bashrc is sourced only for non-login interactive shells (but often called from.bash_profile)

question: You need to transfer a 50GB database dump between two servers. The network bandwidth is 1Gbps but rsync is only achieving 200Mbps. What could improve transfer speed?

Use scp instead; it's always faster than rsync

Use 'cp' command over NFS; it's faster than rsync in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage for production reliability and controlled rollback for production reliability and controlled rollback

question: A production defect investigation needs clarity on the 'ldd' command do, and why should you never run it on untrusted binaries?

question: After deploying a new application, 'dmesg' shows repeated 'Out of memory: Kill process' messages. The server has 64GB RAM but the application is configured with -Xmx48g. What kernel parameter controls how aggressively the OOM killer acts, and what value disables memory overcommit?

question: A production defect investigation needs clarity on the difference between the 'resident set size' (RSS) and 'virtual memory size' (VSZ) of a process as shown by 'ps aux'?

VSZ is always smaller than RSS because it excludes shared libraries in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage
correct_answer: RSS is the physical memory currently in use by the process; VSZ is the total virtual address space including mapped but not yet allocated pages

question: A production database server has vm.swappiness set to 60 (default). The DBA complains about unpredictable latency spikes. What tuning would you recommend and why?
options: Set vm.swappiness=1 or lower to strongly prefer reclaiming page cache over swapping out application memory, reducing latency spikes
Set vm.swappiness=60 is optimal for all workloads; investigate elsewhere
Set vm.swappiness=100 to maximize I/O throughput
Disable swap entirely with swapoff; this is always the best approach for databases in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement
correct_answer: Set vm.swappiness=1 or lower to strongly prefer reclaiming page cache over swapping out application memory, reducing latency spikes

question: A production defect investigation needs clarity on the 'dirty_ratio' and 'dirty_background_ratio' kernel parameters control in Linux?
options: The threshold of dirty (modified in page cache but not written to disk) pages before processes are blocked vs. when background writeback starts
The ratio of user-space to kernel-space memory allocation
The maximum percentage of swap that can be used before OOM triggers in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage
The percentage of corrupted filesystem blocks before fsck triggers
correct_answer: The threshold of dirty (modified in page cache but not written to disk) pages before processes are blocked vs. when background writeback starts

question: You need to view the last 200 log entries for the nginx service since today, including kernel messages. Which journalctl command achieves this?
options: journalctl -u nginx --since today -n 200
journalctl -u nginx --since today -n 200 -k
tail -200 /var/log/syslog | grep nginx
journalctl --unit=nginx --lines=200 --follow
correct_answer: journalctl -u nginx --since today -n 200

question: A server is running out of file descriptors and 'ulimit -n' shows 1024. You need to increase the limit to 65535 for a specific service running under systemd. Where should you configure this?
options: In the systemd unit file using LimitNOFILE=65535, since systemd-managed services do not inherit limits.conf
By running 'ulimit -n 65535' in the service startup script in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement
Only in /etc/security/limits.conf under the user's entry
In /etc/sysctl.conf using fs.file-max=65535
correct_answer: In the systemd unit file using LimitNOFILE=65535, since systemd-managed services do not inherit limits.conf

question: A production defect investigation needs clarity on the difference between 'systemctl restart' and 'systemctl reload' for a service?
options: 'restart' stops and starts the service (new PID); 'reload' sends a signal (typically SIGHUP) to re-read config without stopping the service
'restart' only works for enabled services; 'reload' works for all in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage
They are functionally identical
'reload' always requires a reboot; 'restart' does not
correct_answer: 'restart' stops and starts the service (new PID); 'reload' sends a signal (typically SIGHUP) to re-read config without stopping the service

question: A service is in 'failed' state after a crash. You want it to automatically restart on failure with a 5-second delay. Which systemd unit directives achieve this?
options: Restart=on-failure and RestartSec=5 in the [Service] section
ExecRestart=/bin/sleep 5 in the [Unit] section in this scenario
WatchdogSec=5 and FailureAction=restart
AutoRestart=true and Delay=5
correct_answer: Restart=on-failure and RestartSec=5 in the [Service] section

question: You suspect a network issue between your application server and a database on port 5432. 'ping' works but connections fail intermittently. Which tool would help you diagnose the TCP-level connectivity and path issues?
options: traceroute db-server && ss -tnp | grep 5432
ping -c 10 db-server in this scenario under production constraints
nslookup db-server
arp -a db-server
correct_answer: traceroute db-server && ss -tnp | grep 5432

question: In systemd, what is the purpose of the 'WantedBy=multi-user.target' directive in the [Install] section?
options: It creates a dependency so the service starts when the system reaches multi-user runlevel (runlevel 3 equivalent), i.e., when 'systemctl enable' is run

It limits the service to only run on multi-CPU systems in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage for production reliability and controlled rollback
It prevents the service from starting during boot
It forces the service to run in single-user mode
correct_answer: It creates a dependency so the service starts when the system reaches multi-user runlevel (runlevel 3 equivalent), i.e., when 'systemctl enable' is run

question: You SSH into a server and find a process using 100% of a single CPU core. 'top' shows it as a zombie process (Z state). What does this mean and how do you clean it up?
options: A zombie is a terminated process whose exit status hasn't been collected by its parent; kill the parent process or send SIGCHLD to the parent to reap it - you cannot kill a zombie directly since it's already dead
Use 'renice -20 <pid>' to reduce its CPU usage

Reboot the server; zombies cannot be removed otherwise in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage for production reliability and controlled rollback for production reliability and controlled rollback
Kill the zombie with 'kill -9 <zombie-pid>'
correct_answer: A zombie is a terminated process whose exit status hasn't been collected by its parent; kill the parent process or send SIGCHLD to the parent to reap it - you cannot kill a zombie directly since it's already dead

question: A production defect investigation needs clarity on the difference between SIGTERM (signal 15), SIGKILL (signal 9), and SIGHUP (signal 1)?
options: SIGTERM requests graceful termination (can be caught/handled); SIGKILL forces immediate termination (cannot be caught); SIGHUP traditionally signals config reload or terminal hangup (can be caught)
SIGKILL is the gentlest signal; SIGTERM is the harshest
All three signals immediately terminate the process in the same way

SIGTERM is for terminals; SIGKILL is for kernel processes; SIGHUP is for network connections in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage for production reliability and controlled rollback
correct_answer: SIGTERM requests graceful termination (can be caught/handled); SIGKILL forces immediate termination (cannot be caught); SIGHUP traditionally signals config reload or terminal hangup (can be caught)

question: In a production scenario, which option best describes the difference between iptables chains INPUT, FORWARD, and OUTPUT?
options: INPUT processes packets destined for the local system; FORWARD handles packets being routed through; OUTPUT handles locally generated outbound packets
INPUT handles TCP; FORWARD handles UDP; OUTPUT handles ICMP
They all process the same packets but in different priority order in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage
INPUT handles all packets; FORWARD and OUTPUT are aliases
correct_answer: INPUT processes packets destined for the local system; FORWARD handles packets being routed through; OUTPUT handles locally generated outbound packets

question: You need to run a long-running process that survives after you disconnect from SSH. What are the differences between nohup, screen, tmux, and disown?
options: nohup prevents SIGHUP on disconnect but provides no reattach; screen/tmux create persistent terminal sessions you can reattach to; disown removes a running job from the shell's job table so it won't receive SIGHUP
tmux only works on Ubuntu; screen only works on CentOS
They are all identical tools

screen is deprecated in favor of nohup on all modern systems in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage for production reliability and controlled rollback for production reliability and controlled rollback
correct_answer: nohup prevents SIGHUP on disconnect but provides no reattach; screen/tmux create persistent terminal sessions you can reattach to; disown removes a running job from the shell's job table so it won't receive SIGHUP

question: You need to capture all DNS queries leaving a server to debug name resolution issues. Which tcpdump command would you use?
options: tcpdump -i any port 53 -nn
tcpdump -i eth0 icmp
tcpdump -i lo port 53
tcpdump -i any port 80 in this scenario
correct_answer: tcpdump -i any port 53 -nn

question: A server has multiple network interfaces. You want a service to only listen on the 10.0.1.5 interface, not on all interfaces. How is this typically achieved?
options: Bind the service to 10.0.1.5 instead of 0.0.0.0 in its configuration
Set the default gateway to 10.0.1.5
Remove all other network interfaces using ip link delete in this scenario
Configure iptables to block other interfaces
correct_answer: Bind the service to 10.0.1.5 instead of 0.0.0.0 in its configuration

question: The 'ss -s' command shows 15,000 sockets in TIME_WAIT state on a high-traffic web server. What does this indicate and what kernel parameter can help?
options: These are closed connections waiting for late packets; enable net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse=1 to allow reuse of TIME_WAIT sockets for new outbound connections
All 15,000 connections are actively transferring data
Increase the number of network interfaces to distribute the load in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage
The server has a memory leak; restart all services
correct_answer: These are closed connections waiting for late packets; enable net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse=1 to allow reuse of TIME_WAIT sockets for new outbound connections

question: You run 'curl -v https://api.internal.company.com' and get 'SSL: certificate subject name does not match target host name'. The IP resolves correctly. What is the issue?
options: The certificate's Subject Alternative Name (SAN) or Common Name (CN) does not include 'api.internal.company.com'
The CA bundle is missing on the client
The server is using HTTP/2 which is incompatible in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement
The SSL certificate is expired
correct_answer: The certificate's Subject Alternative Name (SAN) or Common Name (CN) does not include 'api.internal.company.com'

question: During live operations, which option best describes the significance of the setuid (SUID) bit on a Linux executable, and why is it a security concern?
options: It causes the executable to run with the permissions of the file owner (often root) regardless of who executes it, creating potential privilege escalation risks
It prevents the file from being modified after creation
It allows the file to be executed by any user but with no special privileges in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage
It encrypts the file contents during execution
correct_answer: It causes the executable to run with the permissions of the file owner (often root) regardless of who executes it, creating potential privilege escalation risks

question: During a security audit, you need to find all SUID binaries on the system. Which command accomplishes this?
options: find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
chmod -R 4000 /
locate --suid
ls -la /usr/bin | grep suid in this scenario
correct_answer: find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null

question: You need to restrict SSH access to only key-based authentication and disable root login. Which sshd_config directives should you set?
options: PasswordAuthentication no, PermitRootLogin no, PubkeyAuthentication yes
AuthenticationMethods password, RootLogin disabled in this scenario under production constraints
UseSSHKeys only, DisableRoot true
AllowPassword yes, PermitRootLogin yes
correct_answer: PasswordAuthentication no, PermitRootLogin no, PubkeyAuthentication yes

question: During architecture review, which option best describes the 'sticky bit' on a directory (e.g., /tmp with permissions drwxrwxrwt) accomplish?
options: Only the file owner, directory owner, or root can delete or rename files within it, even though others have write permission to the directory
It causes files to be automatically encrypted
It prevents any new files from being created
It makes the directory read-only for all users in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage
correct_answer: Only the file owner, directory owner, or root can delete or rename files within it, even though others have write permission to the directory

question: A compromised server had an attacker add a cron job. Where should you check for persistence mechanisms beyond /etc/crontab?
options: Check /var/spool/cron/crontabs/, /etc/cron.d/, /etc/cron.{daily,weekly,monthly}/, user crontabs, systemd timers, and ~/.bashrc/.profile for backdoors
Only check /tmp for suspicious scripts
Only /etc/crontab needs to be checked
Run 'crontab -l' as root only; that shows everything in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage for production reliability and controlled rollback
correct_answer: Check /var/spool/cron/crontabs/, /etc/cron.d/, /etc/cron.{daily,weekly,monthly}/, user crontabs, systemd timers, and ~/.bashrc/.profile for backdoors

question: What is the purpose of SELinux 'enforcing' mode and how does it differ from 'permissive' mode?
options: Enforcing mode actively blocks policy violations and logs them; permissive mode only logs violations without blocking, useful for testing policies
Enforcing mode disables all user access; permissive mode allows root-only access
Enforcing mode logs violations but allows them; permissive mode blocks all access in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement
Both modes are identical in behavior
correct_answer: Enforcing mode actively blocks policy violations and logs them; permissive mode only logs violations without blocking, useful for testing policies

question: In a production scenario, which option best describes the 'nice' value of a process control, and what is the range of nice values on Linux?
options: Nice controls CPU scheduling priority; range is -20 (highest priority) to 19 (lowest priority); only root can set negative nice values
Nice controls memory allocation priority; range is 1 to 10 in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage
Nice controls network bandwidth; range is -100 to 100
Nice controls I/O priority; range is 0 to 100
correct_answer: Nice controls CPU scheduling priority; range is -20 (highest priority) to 19 (lowest priority); only root can set negative nice values

question: You need to create a filesystem on /dev/sdb1, mount it at /data with noexec and nosuid options, and make it persist across reboots. What is the correct sequence?
options: mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdb1; mkdir -p /data; mount -o noexec,nosuid /dev/sdb1 /data; add entry to /etc/fstab with options noexec,nosuid
parted /dev/sdb1 mkfs; mount /dev/sdb1 /data; crontab @reboot mount
fdisk /dev/sdb1 --format; mountpoint /data; echo mount >> /etc/rc.local in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement
format /dev/sdb1; mount /data; edit /etc/mtab
correct_answer: mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdb1; mkdir -p /data; mount -o noexec,nosuid /dev/sdb1 /data; add entry to /etc/fstab with options noexec,nosuid

question: A production server shows a filesystem at 100% usage but 'du -sh /*' reports only 60% used. What could explain this discrepancy?
options: Deleted files are still held open by running processes, consuming space not visible to du; use 'lsof +L1' to find them
The 'du' command is inaccurate on ext4 filesystems
The filesystem has bad sectors hiding the actual usage
Reserved blocks for root (typically 5%) account for 40% of disk space in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement
correct_answer: Deleted files are still held open by running processes, consuming space not visible to du; use 'lsof +L1' to find them

question: What is the key difference between ext4 and XFS filesystems in a production Linux environment?
options: XFS excels at parallel I/O with large files and cannot be shrunk; ext4 supports both grow and shrink operations and is more versatile for general use
ext4 is deprecated in all modern distributions in favor of XFS
XFS does not support journaling while ext4 does
ext4 supports only files up to 1GB; XFS supports unlimited file sizes in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage
correct_answer: XFS excels at parallel I/O with large files and cannot be shrunk; ext4 supports both grow and shrink operations and is more versatile for general use

question: You need to extend a logical volume /dev/vg0/lv_data by 50GB and resize the ext4 filesystem online (without unmounting). What commands would you use?
options: lvextend -L +50G /dev/vg0/lv_data && resize2fs /dev/vg0/lv_data
vgextend +50G vg0 && mkfs.ext4 /dev/vg0/lv_data
fdisk /dev/vg0/lv_data --resize +50G
lvresize -r -L 50G /dev/vg0/lv_data (this shrinks to 50G, not extends by 50G)
correct_answer: lvextend -L +50G /dev/vg0/lv_data && resize2fs /dev/vg0/lv_data

question: During architecture review, which option best describes the 'inode' represent in a Linux filesystem, and what happens when all inodes are exhausted even though disk space is available?
options: An inode stores metadata (permissions, ownership, timestamps, block pointers) for a file; when inodes are exhausted, no new files or directories can be created despite free disk space
Inodes are network indexes; exhausting them blocks network access

Inodes store file data blocks; exhausting them corrupts existing files in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage for production reliability and controlled rollback
Inodes are only relevant to NFS filesystems
correct_answer: An inode stores metadata (permissions, ownership, timestamps, block pointers) for a file; when inodes are exhausted, no new files or directories can be created despite free disk space

question: You need to write a bash script that processes a CSV file, extracts the 3rd column, sorts unique values, and counts occurrences. Which pipeline accomplishes this?
options: awk -F',' '{print $3}' file.csv | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
cat file.csv | grep -c column3
sed 's/,/\n/g' file.csv | head -3 in this scenario under production constraints
cut -f3 file.csv | sort | wc -l
correct_answer: awk -F',' '{print $3}' file.csv | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

question: What is the difference between $*, $@, "$*", and "$@" in bash when a script is called with arguments 'arg1' 'arg 2' 'arg3'?
options: "$@" preserves each argument as a separate quoted word (3 args); "$*" combines all arguments into a single string; unquoted versions are subject to word splitting
$* preserves quotes while $@ does not
$@ only works inside functions; $* works everywhere in this scenario under production constraints for the stated requirement during incident triage for production reliability and controlled rollback
They are all identical and interchangeable
correct_answer: "$@" preserves each argument as a separate quoted word (3 args); "$*" combines all arguments into a single string; unquoted versions are subject to word splitting
